title: Inspect Exact Project Changes
summary: Continue community-site and inspect supplied text edits before deciding what belongs in history.

sketchBlocks.title: Inspect unstaged edits with git diff
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: Suppose you have a small project and you changed two files but haven't staged anything yet. Running git diff shows the exact lines added and removed in your working tree. Example output (simplified):

```
diff --git a/team.txt b/team.txt
index e69de29..a1b2c3d 100644
--- a/team.txt
+++ b/team.txt
@@ -0,0 +1,2 @@
+Alice — Community lead
+Ben — Outreach coordinator
diff --git a/contact.md b/contact.md
index e69de29..d4e5f6a 100644
--- a/contact.md
+++ b/contact.md
@@ -1,3 +1,4 @@
-Email: info@example.org
+Email: hello@example.org
+Phone: (555) 123-4567
```

Interpretation: lines beginning with + are additions; lines with - are removals. The file header lines (diff --git ...) show which files changed. Use git diff before staging so you know exactly which text will be included if you stage everything.

Step by step, read the commands in the order shown. The first line runs `diff --git a/team.txt b/team.txt` as the next repository step in the example. The second line runs `index e69de29..a1b2c3d 100644` as the next repository step in the example. The third line runs `--- a/team.txt` as the next repository step in the example. The fourth line runs `+++ b/team.txt` as the next repository step in the example. The next line runs `@@ -0,0 +1,2 @@` as the next repository step in the example. The next line runs `+Alice — Community lead` as the next repository step in the example. The next line runs `+Ben — Outreach coordinator` as the next repository step in the example. The next line runs `diff --git a/contact.md b/contact.md` as the next repository step in the example. The next line runs `index e69de29..d4e5f6a 100644` as the next repository step in the example. The next line runs `--- a/contact.md` as the next repository step in the example. The next line runs `+++ b/contact.md` as the next repository step in the example. The next line runs `@@ -1,3 +1,4 @@` as the next repository step in the example. The next line runs `-Email: info@example.org` as the next repository step in the example. The next line runs `+Email: hello@example.org` as the next repository step in the example. The next line runs `+Phone: (555) 123-4567` as the next repository step in the example. Together, the lines form one complete example rather than unrelated commands.

sketchBlocks.title: Limit inspection to one file
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: When several files changed, prefer limiting inspection to the file you care about. Use git diff <path> to see only that file's hunks. Example command and output:

```
$ git diff docs/privacy.txt
diff --git a/docs/privacy.txt b/docs/privacy.txt
index 9f8e7d6..7c6b5a4 100644
--- a/docs/privacy.txt
+++ b/docs/privacy.txt
@@ -10,7 +10,7 @@ Data we collect:
- We collect email addresses for newsletters.
+ We collect email addresses for announcements and support.
```

Why this helps: focusing on one file keeps you from being distracted by unrelated edits and makes the staging decision precise.

sketchBlocks.title: Understand hunks and context lines
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: A diff hunk shows a few lines of surrounding context so you can see the change in place. Example hunk:

```
@@ -2,3 +2,4 @@ Welcome to our site.
-Please send feedback to team@old.org
+Please send feedback to team@new.org
+We now also accept in-person volunteers.
```

Here the @@ header shows the approximate location in the file; the - line is what was removed and the + lines are the new content. Context lines (no prefix) help you verify the surrounding text so you don't accidentally stage unrelated edits.

sketchBlocks.title: Compare unstaged diffs with staged diffs
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: After you stage a file with git add, git diff shows only unstaged changes while git diff --staged (aka --cached) shows what is ready to commit. Example command sequence and conceptual output:

```
$ git add docs/guide.md
$ git diff            # shows remaining unstaged edits in other files
$ git diff --staged  # shows the snapshot of docs/guide.md that will be committed
```

Common mistake: running git add and then assuming everything changed will be committed. Always run git diff and git diff --staged as appropriate to confirm the exact snapshot that will become history.

quizDraft.title: What does git diff show by default?
quizDraft.prompt: By default, what does git diff (with no options) show?
options: Only the changes that are staged and ready to commit.
All unstaged working-tree edits compared to the index (what you've changed but not staged).
The entire commit history with one-line summaries.
A list of untracked files in the working directory.
quizDraft.hint: Consider whether git diff shows staged changes or unstaged working-tree edits.

help.concept: git diff (no flags) displays differences between the working tree and the index (unstaged edits). It does not show what is already staged for commit.
help.hint_1: It compares working tree vs index (unstaged changes).
help.hint_2: Pick the option that says unstaged working-tree edits, not staged snapshots.

quizDraft.title: When to use git diff
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following are good reasons to run git diff before staging or committing? (Choose all that apply.)
options: To verify exactly which lines were added or removed before including them in history.
To confirm that a staged snapshot contains only the intended changes.
To permanently delete a file from history.
To avoid adding unrelated whitespace or debug text into a commit message.
quizDraft.hint: Think about verifying exact text changes and avoiding unrelated edits in history.

help.concept: git diff reveals exact added/removed lines so you can confirm that staged commits capture one clear purpose and exclude accidental edits.
help.hint_1: Consider preventing unrelated edits from entering a commit and verifying the text your commit would contain.
help.hint_2: Select all choices that describe verifying change contents or preventing accidental staging.

quizDraft.title: Interpret diff symbols
quizDraft.prompt: In a git diff hunk, what do lines starting with + and - mean? (Choose all that apply.)
options: A + line indicates content that was added in the working tree compared to HEAD.
A - line indicates content that will be added when committing.
A - line indicates content that was removed from the working tree compared to HEAD.
A + line indicates context and is unchanged.
quizDraft.hint: Focus on which symbol means addition vs removal.

help.concept: In diffs, + lines are new additions and - lines are removed; context lines have no prefix.
help.hint_2: Choose both statements that correctly describe + and -.

quizDraft.title: Correct sequence to inspect and prepare a focused commit
quizDraft.prompt: Put these steps in the correct order to inspect an unstaged change and prepare a focused commit:
tokens: Run git diff to read unstaged changes
Stage the targeted file(s) with git add
Run git diff --staged to view the snapshot to be committed
Write a focused commit message and git commit
correctOrder: Run git diff to read unstaged changes
Stage the targeted file(s) with git add
Run git diff --staged to view the snapshot to be committed
Write a focused commit message and git commit
quizDraft.hint: Think: inspect, stage only the wanted file, then confirm staged snapshot.

help.concept: Best practice: inspect unstaged edits with git diff, stage only the file(s) that belong together, then inspect the staged snapshot with git diff --staged before committing.
help.hint_1: First inspect unstaged edits, then stage targeted files, then inspect staged snapshot.
help.hint_2: The final step before commit is to check git diff --staged to confirm what's ready to commit.

quizDraft.title: Choose the command to see staged snapshot
quizDraft.prompt: To see only what is staged and will be in the next commit, use [blank1].
quizDraft.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Pick the choice that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

quizDraft.title: Command to view one file's unstaged diff
quizDraft.prompt: To inspect only the file community-site/README.md, run: git diff [blank1]
quizDraft.hint: Fill in the blank with how to name the file path on the command line.

help.concept: git diff accepts a path argument to limit the displayed hunks to that file path.
help.hint_1: Put the relative path to the file in the blank.
help.hint_2: The correct answer is the missing term

quizDraft.title: View all unstaged edits with git diff
quizDraft.prompt: You are in the community-site repository. Two files have unstaged text edits: community-site/index.html and community-site/ABOUT.md. Type git diff and press Enter to view the unstaged changes across the repository. (Use the terminal; type the command and press Enter.) Then click Check Answer.
